statement.markdown: Implement a stack using queue(s). The stack must support standard operations: push (push element x onto stack), pop (remove and return the top element), and top (return the top element without removing it). If the stack is empty, pop and top should return -1.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer Q, representing the number of queries.
Each of the next Q lines contains a query: either 'push x', 'pop', or 'top'.
statement.outputFormat: For each 'pop' or 'top' query, print the returned value on a new line.

hints.text: To implement a stack using queues, think about how you can utilize the FIFO nature of queues to mimic the LIFO behavior of a stack. Consider using two queues and how elements can be moved between them to achieve the desired order.

One approach to solving this problem is to use one queue as the main storage and the other queue to help achieve the LIFO order during pop operations. Think about how you can rotate or move elements between these two queues to ensure the most recently added element is always the first one to be removed.

For the push operation, it's straightforward: simply enqueue the new element into the main queue. For the pop operation, consider how you can use the second queue to temporarily hold elements from the main queue, except for the last element which will be dequeued and returned as the result of the pop operation. Think about the process of rotating elements between the two queues to maintain the correct order.

editorial.title: Implementing Stack using Queue: A Complexity Analysis
editorial.intuition: The problem requires implementing a stack using two queues. A stack follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, whereas a queue follows the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle. The key observation is that we can use two queues to mimic the behavior of a stack by cleverly managing the elements in the queues.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve iterating through each operation (push, pop, top) and performing the corresponding action. However, this approach would be slow because it would not optimize the use of the queues, potentially leading to inefficient operations.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves using two queues, q1 and q2. When pushing an element, we append it to q2 and then move all elements from q1 to q2. We then swap q1 and q2. This ensures that the most recently added element is always at the front of q1, allowing for efficient pop and top operations.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), where n is the number of operations. This is because in the worst-case scenario, we might need to move all elements from one queue to the other for each operation.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it maintains the LIFO property of a stack. When pushing an element, it is added to the end of q2 and then moved to the front of q1 through the swapping process. When popping or accessing the top element, we simply remove or return the front element of q1, ensuring that the most recently added element is always processed first.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with sample inputs (e.g., push 1, push 2, pop, top) verifies that the solution behaves as expected, processing operations in O(n) time and using O(n) space.
commonMistakes: Not swapping the queues after pushing an element, leading to incorrect ordering.
Not checking if a queue is empty before popping or accessing the top element, leading to errors.
Using only one queue, which cannot efficiently implement a stack.
Not considering the amortized time complexity, which can lead to an incorrect assessment of the solution's efficiency.
Incorrectly handling edge cases, such as popping from an empty stack.

functionSignature.cpp: void push(int x); int pop(); int top();
functionSignature.java: public void push(int x); public int pop(); public int top();
functionSignature.python: def push(self, x: int) -> None; def pop(self) -> int; def top(self) -> int;
